metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the plan-review error-dispatch spec with `review_plan`'s actual behavior: since `LLMUnavailableError` is absorbed internally into `INDETERMINATE`, specify whether the service should treat `INDETERMINATE` from a plan-review job as `status='error'` (with `error.class='llm_unavailable'`) or as a distinct terminal verdict, and update the AC and error-class mapping accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'A raised `LLMError`/`LLMUnavailableError` maps to the job's transport status — never a fabricated verdict' and specifies `error.class = "llm_unavailable"` for `LLMUnavailableError`. For the `plan-review` kind this premise is false: `rebar.llm.review_plan` absorbs `LLMUnavailableError` internally (inside `produce_plan_review_verdict`, which calls `_degraded_plan_review_verdict` and returns `INDETERMINATE`) and its docstring explicitly states 'Raises only on a hard context-assembly failure; an unavailable LLM degrades to a DET-only review.' The worker's `except LLMUnavailableError` block will never fire for a `plan-review` job — LLM failures will reach the service as `status='completed', verdict='INDETERMINATE'` rather than `status='error'` as the plan intends. Only `verify_completion` raises `LLMError` to the caller.
findings.location: What section — 'Per-kind dispatch + native verdicts' paragraph; and AC 'Per-kind verdict mapping' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: For plan-review: inspect the returned verdict's `verdict` field and `coverage.llm_ran` rather than catching a raised exception. Map `INDETERMINATE` with `coverage.llm_ran=False` (systemic LLM failure) to `status='error', error.class='llm_unavailable'`; map `INDETERMINATE` with `coverage.llm_ran=True` (partial run) to `status='completed', verdict='INDETERMINATE'`. Only `verify_completion` needs the raised-exception catch.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` and `src/rebar/_cli/help/remote-cert.txt` to the edit-set (the intercept-ladder arm and help file that make `rebar remote-cert` a recognized subcommand).
findings.finding: The plan creates `src/rebar/_commands/remote_cert.py` and the CLI command `rebar remote-cert` but does not enumerate the required wiring in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` (a new `if argv[0] == 'remote-cert':` intercept-ladder entry) nor the `src/rebar/_cli/help/remote-cert.txt` help file that `known_subcommands()` uses to recognize the command. Without these, `rebar remote-cert` will be rejected as an unknown subcommand at runtime.
findings.location: Client section / `src/rebar/_commands/remote_cert.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope/In list: `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` (new `if argv[0] == 'remote-cert':` intercept arm) and `src/rebar/_cli/help/remote-cert.txt` (pinned help text for `known_subcommands()`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` to the edit-set (a new `'remote-cert': '...'` entry in `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS`) so the CLI-reference drift gate remains green.
findings.finding: `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` is an unenumerated consumer: it parses the `argv[0] ==` intercept ladder in `_cli/__init__.py` and does a strict set-equality check against its curated `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS` dict. Adding a `remote-cert` arm to the ladder without also adding it to `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS` raises `ValueError` and fails `make test`.
findings.location: Client section / `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` to Scope/In with a one-line description entry for `remote-cert` in the `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS` dict.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/env-vars.md` (regenerated via `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py`) to the edit-set so the env-var drift gate stays green.
findings.finding: All new `REBAR_OPCERT_*` environment variables read inside `src/rebar/opcert_service/` will be auto-detected by `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` (its scan root is `src/rebar`), causing the CI env-var drift gate to fail `make test` until `docs/env-vars.md` is regenerated. The plan neither mentions regenerating `docs/env-vars.md` nor lists it as an edit target.
findings.location: Service section (env vars: REBAR_OPCERT_JOB_TIMEOUT_SECONDS, REBAR_OPCERT_REVIEW_REMOTE_URL, REBAR_OPCERT_TICKETS_REMOTE_URL, REBAR_OPCERT_KEY_PATH, REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID, REBAR_OPCERT_GUARD)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope/In: '`docs/env-vars.md` (regenerate with `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py` after adding the REBAR_OPCERT_* reads)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (in this plan or via explicit seam contract) that the producer signing seam adds `sign=True` (or equivalent) support to `verify_completion`, not just `review_plan`, so the CV path also appends a SIGNATURE event the worker can extract.
findings.finding: The `completion-verifier` dispatch path's envelope-extraction mechanism silently depends on the producer seam adding `sign=True` support to `verify_completion`, but the live code confirms `verify_completion` has no `sign` parameter (unlike `review_plan`'s `sign: bool = True`). The plan says the seam 'must land first' but never confirms that the seam story adds a signing path to `verify_completion`. If the seam only signs `review_plan`, the CV dispatch produces no SIGNATURE event in the ephemeral workspace, and the envelope extraction step ('the worker extracts the envelope from the workspace's freshly-appended record') fails silently or crashes — breaking the CV path entirely.
findings.location: What > Service > Per-kind dispatch; What > Service > Signing = the producer seam
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the 'Signing = the producer seam' section: 'The seam story MUST add signing support to both `review_plan` (already has `sign: bool = True`) AND `verify_completion` (currently unsigned); the CV dispatch path requires a freshly-appended SIGNATURE event in the ephemeral workspace to extract the envelope.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the envelope extraction protocol: name the return-dict field (or store event query) the worker uses to retrieve the encoded DSSE envelope from a completed `review_plan` / `verify_completion` call, so the AC 'envelope present ONLY on PASS' is implementable without inferring the seam's internal shape.
findings.finding: The envelope extraction protocol for completed jobs is under-specified. The plan says 'the worker extracts the envelope from the workspace's freshly-appended record', but does not name the store event type, the dict field, or the lookup mechanism for either kind — and for `completion-verifier` the live `verify_completion()` has no `sign` parameter and its return schema has no `signature` or `envelope` field, so the extraction path depends entirely on the (not-yet-landed) producer-seam story's shape, which is not described here.
findings.location: What section — Service, 'Stateless / store-read-only — reconciled with the seam's `sign=True`'; also 'the worker extracts the envelope from the workspace's freshly-appended record'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC7's proving command: either (a) pin the package to `openssh-client>=1:8.9` in the Dockerfile and assert the pinned version string in the test, or (b) add a Dockerfile `RUN ssh-keygen --version` smoke step that fails the build if the version is below 8.9, making the image build itself the proving command.
findings.finding: AC7 states 'a test asserts the Dockerfile declares both' (OpenSSH ≥ 8.9 installation and service startup), but asserting a declaration in Dockerfile text does not verify the actual built container runs OpenSSH ≥ 8.9 — a parse of `RUN apt-get install openssh-client` with any version string would satisfy the test even if the pinned version is < 8.9. This is a fixture-validity gap (verify-command defect type 4).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert installs OpenSSH ≥ 8.9 and starts the service; a test asserts the Dockerfile declares both.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Dockerfile: `RUN ssh-keygen -V 2>&1 | grep -E 'OpenSSH_([89]\.[9-9]|[9-9]|[1-9][0-9])' || (echo 'OpenSSH >= 8.9 required' && exit 1)` — this makes the Docker build itself the falsifiable version check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly declare the access class of `GET /opcert/jobs/{job_id}` (e.g., 'same X-Opcert-Guard + SigV4 required as POST') and add an AC line asserting a guard-missing GET request is also rejected 403.
findings.finding: The access posture of GET /opcert/jobs/{job_id} is undeclared: the plan says 'the app additionally requires a shared secret' (implying app-wide middleware) but the guard AC is scoped to 'before any work is enqueued' (POST path only). It is ambiguous whether the GET status endpoint enforces X-Opcert-Guard. Job status responses include DSSE envelopes, verdicts, and material fingerprints — information that should not be accessible without authentication. If the guard is middleware it covers both; if it is a POST-only decorator it does not.
findings.location: AC: 'Origin guard' bullet; What section: 'GET /opcert/jobs/{job_id} returns the job record'; 'the app additionally requires a shared secret'
findings.suggested_fix: Apply the X-Opcert-Guard check as FastAPI middleware (not a per-route dependency) so it fires for every request regardless of method, and add a single AC assertion: 'GET /opcert/jobs/{id} without X-Opcert-Guard returns 403'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID` to the service env-var table with an explicit source (e.g. 'set as a deploy-time env var, materialized from SSM `/rebar/prod/opcert-env-id` by the deploy story') so the executor knows how the worker obtains the value.
findings.finding: `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID` is used by the worker as the principal injected into the gate-run subprocess environment, but the plan never lists it as a service-level environment variable that must be present in the service's own runtime environment. An executor implementing the service will not know where this value comes from (SSM? hardcoded? deploy-time env var?).
findings.location: What > Signing section: 'sets REBAR_OPCERT_KEY_PATH to that path and REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID as the principal for the gate run'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the env-var list: '`REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID`: the logical environment id used as the DSSE keyid / SSHSIG principal; a deploy-time env var set by the IaC story (e.g. `nava-opcert-prod-1`); the worker passes it to the gate-run subprocess as-is.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific Python library for SigV4 client signing in `remote_cert.py` (e.g. `botocore.auth.SigV4Auth` + `botocore.awsrequest.AWSPreparedRequest`, or a thin wrapper like `aws-requests-auth`/`httpx-auth`) and declare it in the appropriate optional extra — both the POST job submission and GET polling calls must be signed.
findings.finding: The client command is described as 'SigV4-signs the request with ambient AWS credentials' but no library or implementation approach for SigV4 HTTP signing is named. There is zero SigV4 client precedent in the codebase (the existing review_bot uses a plain `?token=` query param; no aws_requests_auth/botocore.awsrequest/httpx-auth-sigv4 import exists anywhere). This is an unverified capability assertion: the correct signing library and its dependency declaration (which extra it would belong to) are unresolved.
findings.location: Client section: 'SigV4-signs the request with ambient AWS credentials'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the SigV4 client signing mechanism (e.g., `botocore.auth.SigV4Auth`, `requests-aws4auth`, or manual HMAC-SHA256) and add it — plus any required library — to a declared `pyproject.toml` extra for the `rebar remote-cert` command.
findings.finding: The client command `rebar remote-cert` is specified to 'SigV4-sign the request with ambient AWS credentials', but no SigV4 signing library is declared in `pyproject.toml` (no `requests-aws4auth`, `aws-requests-auth`, `botocore` standalone signer, or equivalent). `boto3` appears only in the `reviewbot` extra and is not available on the core client path. SigV4 HMAC-SHA256 request signing requires either a library or substantial manual construction — neither is named.
findings.location: Client section: 'SigV4-signs the request with ambient AWS credentials'; `pyproject.toml` optional-dependencies
findings.suggested_fix: Use `botocore.auth.SigV4Auth` (available as a transitive of `boto3`): declare a new `opcert` extra `['fastapi', 'uvicorn[standard]', 'boto3']` (mirroring `reviewbot`) and document that the client path uses `botocore.auth.SigV4Auth` + `botocore.awsrequest.AWSRequest` for signing, driven by the ambient session credentials.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the client polling interval and max-wait ceiling in the client spec (e.g. 'poll every 5 s up to 960 s, then exit non-zero with `timeout`').
findings.finding: The client command `rebar remote-cert` is specified to 'poll to a terminal status' but no polling interval or client-side timeout is stated. An executor must make an arbitrary implementation decision about how frequently to poll and when to give up — a defaultable gap that should be stated explicitly.
findings.location: Client section: 'submits the job, polls to a terminal status'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the client spec: 'polls every `REBAR_OPCERT_POLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS` (default 5 s) up to `REBAR_OPCERT_CLIENT_TIMEOUT_SECONDS` (default 960 s, i.e. server ceiling + one poll interval), then exits non-zero with error class `timeout`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the client's polling strategy: initial delay, interval or backoff schedule, and maximum client-side wait duration (e.g. 'poll every 5 s with exponential backoff up to 30 s, ceiling = REBAR_OPCERT_JOB_TIMEOUT_SECONDS + margin'), and add an AC covering poll-timeout/server-unreachable behavior.
findings.finding: The client's polling strategy for the async job is unspecified: interval, backoff, and maximum wait duration are not named, leaving the developer to guess behavior for operations that may run 30 seconds to minutes.
findings.location: Client section: 'submits the job, polls to a terminal status'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Client section: 'Polls GET /opcert/jobs/{job_id} with 5 s initial delay, exponential backoff (cap 30 s), for up to REBAR_OPCERT_JOB_TIMEOUT_SECONDS + 60 s; if the server is unreachable or the ceiling is exceeded, exits non-zero with class `timeout` or `network_error` respectively.' Add a corresponding AC asserting this behavior.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the mechanism for in-process SSM key fetch in the service worker (e.g. boto3 `ssm.get_parameter(WithDecryption=True)`) and declare its dependency in the opcert extra or Dockerfile pip install line — the shell-script pattern from `fetch-secrets.sh` cannot be directly reused inside an async job worker.
findings.finding: The service worker is described as 'fetches the SSM SecureString (`/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`)' in-process at job dispatch time, but no Python library is named for this SSM call. The codebase's only SSM fetch precedent is the shell script `fetch-secrets.sh` which uses the AWS CLI (`aws ssm get-parameter`), not in-process boto3. While boto3 is in the `reviewbot` extra, there is no Python SSM fetch code anywhere in the repo. The plan does not state whether boto3 is reused, a new `opcert` extra is declared, or a different approach (e.g. calling the same shell script pattern) is used.
findings.location: Service / Signing section: 'the worker fetches the SSM SecureString (`/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a named `opcert` (or `opcertservice`) extra to `pyproject.toml` declaring `[fastapi, uvicorn[standard], boto3]` and reference it explicitly in the `Dockerfile.opcert` `pip install` line.
findings.finding: The plan creates a new `src/rebar/opcert_service/` package and `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` that will need a `pip install .[<extras>]` invocation, but no new `opcert` (or equivalent) extra is declared in `pyproject.toml`. The `reviewbot` extra (`fastapi`, `uvicorn[standard]`, `boto3`) is a plausible superset, but the plan does not name it as the target extra for the opcert service install, leaving the Dockerfile's `pip install` line unspecified.
findings.location: `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` (described in plan); `pyproject.toml` optional-dependencies
findings.suggested_fix: Add to pyproject.toml: `opcert = ["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "boto3"]` and specify `pip install ".[agents,opcert]"` in Dockerfile.opcert (mirroring the reviewbot pattern of `.[agents,reviewbot]`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that asserts `rebar remote-cert` SigV4-signs outbound requests using ambient AWS credentials (e.g. a test that captures the outgoing request and verifies the `Authorization` header or the signing stub was invoked).
findings.finding: The client's SigV4-signing of requests is described as a first-class deliverable in the Client section ('SigV4-signs the request with ambient AWS credentials') but no AC covers or verifies this behavior. A client that omits SigV4 signing would pass all ACs yet fail at the API Gateway layer.
findings.location: Client section ('SigV4-signs the request with ambient AWS credentials') — no corresponding AC

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC3 (or add a sibling AC) to assert each error.class value: LLMUnavailableError → 'llm_unavailable', other LLMError → 'llm_error', per-run timeout → 'timeout', and unexpected exception → 'internal'.
findings.finding: The `error.class` closed enum (four values: `llm_unavailable`, `llm_error`, `timeout`, `internal`) is a described schema element in the What section, but AC3 only partially covers it: it asserts `status="error"` with `verdict=null` for a raised LLMError, but no AC verifies the specific `error.class` string values, the `timeout` path (per-run timeout exceeded), or the `internal` catch-all.
findings.location: What section — response schema 'error.class is a CLOSED enum…'; AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC 5) with tests asserting `rebar remote-cert` exits non-zero and surfaces the `error.class` / verdict when the service returns `status='error'`, `BLOCK`, `FAIL`, or `INDETERMINATE` — covering at least one non-PASS verdict path and one error-status path for the client command.
findings.finding: The `rebar remote-cert` client command has no acceptance criterion testing its behavior when the service returns a non-PASS terminal status (BLOCK, FAIL, INDETERMINATE) or `status='error'`. The prose specifies 'Non-PASS / error → non-zero exit with the job's error class' but no AC enforces this via a test, leaving the client's failure-path contract unverified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 5 (rebar remote-cert); 'What' section, Client paragraph: 'Non-PASS / error → non-zero exit with the job's error class'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC 5: 'a job returning status=error exits non-zero and prints the error.class; a job returning a non-PASS verdict (e.g. BLOCK for plan-review, FAIL for completion-verifier) exits non-zero with the verdict surfaced to stderr; a PASS job exits 0 and persists the envelope.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit note in the plan that WORKER_COUNT MUST remain 1 as a correctness invariant (not just a throughput choice) because `REBAR_OPCERT_KEY_PATH` is a process-global env var; if parallel workers are ever needed, per-worker isolated env (e.g. `subprocess.Popen(env=...)`) or a different key-delivery mechanism is required.
findings.finding: The plan mandates a single background worker to 'drain serially' but never states that WORKER_COUNT=1 is also a correctness constraint required for key-path isolation. Setting `REBAR_OPCERT_KEY_PATH` is a process-global side-effect; with WORKER_COUNT>1, two concurrent workers would race — Job A's finally-delete could remove Job B's key file mid-sign, or vice versa. The plan's positive rationale for single-worker only cites throughput/serialization, not safety.
findings.location: What > Service > Async job API; What > Service > Signing = the producer seam
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Async job API' or 'Signing' section add: 'WORKER_COUNT is fixed at 1 as a CORRECTNESS constraint, not merely throughput: REBAR_OPCERT_KEY_PATH is a process-global env var; multiple workers would race on key-path isolation. Any future parallelization must pass the key via a job-local mechanism (e.g. per-subprocess env dict) rather than the process environment.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly assign ownership of the `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` SSM SecureString slot (the CHANGEME placeholder + `ignore_changes = [value]` entry in `ssm.tf`) to either this story's Scope or the sibling IaC story's scope, and add a cross-reference so neither story ships without the slot.
findings.finding: The plan's runtime key-fetch references the SSM SecureString `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`, but this parameter name does not appear in `ssm.tf`'s `rebar_secret_params` list and the plan does not specify which story provisions the SSM parameter slot (with the CHANGEME placeholder + `ignore_changes = [value]` lifecycle pattern used for all other secrets). If neither this story nor the sibling IaC story explicitly adds the slot, the service will fail at first key-fetch with a ParameterNotFound error.
findings.location: What section — Service subsection: 'Before dispatching a job, the worker fetches the SSM SecureString (`/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`)'; also infra/terraform/ssm.tf rebar_secret_params list
findings.suggested_fix: Add `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` and `/rebar/prod/opcert-guard` to `ssm.tf`'s `rebar_secret_params` list in this story (since the service code that consumes them is in scope here), or explicitly note in the Scope section that the IaC story must add these slots and add them to that story's ACs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Merge AC5's 'store unchanged' assertion into AC9 (or vice-versa) so a single criterion covers both the mechanism (REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off, no push credential) and the observable effect (shared store byte-identical), and remove the redundant half.
findings.finding: AC5 and AC9 are near-duplicates: both assert the shared store is unmodified after a job completes. AC5 says 'a test asserts the store is unchanged after a completed job'; AC9 says 'a test asserts… the shared store is byte-identical after a completed PASS job (the SIGNATURE event exists only in the discarded workspace).' The only distinguishing content in AC9 (REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off / no push remote/credential assertions) could be folded into AC5, or one criterion should be pruned.
findings.location: AC5 and AC9
findings.suggested_fix: Consolidate into AC9 (keep the mechanism assertions) and add to it the client-persist + tamper-rejection content currently in AC5; drop the standalone 'store is unchanged' sentence from AC5.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a timing probe AC or RED fixture that records actual gate-run durations (plan-review and completion-verifier) against the fake authoritative source to derive—rather than assert—the 900 s default, or document the empirical basis (e.g. production log samples) in the plan.
findings.finding: The 900 s job timeout ceiling is asserted as 'generous' based on informal observation ('gate runs take 30s–minutes') with no timing probe, benchmark, or red-fixture measuring actual tail-case durations to confirm the ceiling is safe and not wasteful.
findings.location: What / Async job API — 'Single background worker drains serially under a bounded per-run timeout: default 900 s (gate runs take 30s–minutes; the ceiling is generous)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the existing round-trip test AC, add a timing assertion that records wall-clock duration of a representative fake gate run and confirms it falls well below the 900 s ceiling; or cite a sampled percentile from existing gate-run logs as the derivation basis for the 900 s value.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify retry/backoff policy (e.g., 3 attempts with exponential backoff) for the git-fetch and SSM-parameter calls, and document the fallback behaviour when retries are exhausted.
findings.finding: No retry or backoff is specified for the two authoritative-fetch calls (git fetch of tickets branch and review remote main). A transient network hiccup causes an immediate `error` job with no self-healing, and repeated client resubmits could pile up in the queue.
findings.location: What > Authoritative fetch section; AC: POST /opcert/jobs round-trip test
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the git-fetch and `ssm.get_parameter` calls in a helper with configurable retry (e.g., `tenacity` with 3 attempts, 1 s base, 2× backoff); map exhausted-retry to `error.class = "internal"` with a clear log line including the operation name and attempt count.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement: specify that each terminal job state transition (especially `error`) emits a structured log line with `job_id`, `kind`, `error.class`, and elapsed time, and at minimum a counter metric per `error.class` so operators can alert on elevated error rates.
findings.finding: No observability (structured logging, metrics, or alerts) is specified for any failure point in the service — SSM fetch failures, git-fetch failures, LLM errors, job timeouts, and worker crashes are all silent to operators.
findings.location: What section (entire service spec); Acceptance Criteria (no AC covers logging/metrics)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the background worker task is supervised (e.g., wrapped in a restart loop or monitored via `asyncio.Task.add_done_callback`) so an unhandled exception restarts the worker rather than silently stopping job processing.
findings.finding: The background worker coroutine's crash/restart lifecycle is unspecified: if the single asyncio worker task raises an unhandled exception (e.g., from a bug in the dispatch path), it exits silently and all subsequently enqueued jobs are permanently stuck in `queued` state — `GET /opcert/jobs/{id}` never advances.
findings.location: What > Async job API section: 'Single background worker drains serially'
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the worker loop body in `while True: try: await _process_one_job() except Exception: log.exception('worker iteration failed'); await asyncio.sleep(1)` and add an `add_done_callback` on the task that logs CRITICAL and restarts on unexpected exit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a RETIRE path for completed job records: specify a TTL (e.g., 1 hour) or a max-records cap (e.g., 1 000 entries, evict oldest-completed first) so memory usage is bounded in long-running deployments.
findings.finding: In-memory job records have no TTL, eviction policy, or size cap: in a long-running service, every completed/errored job record accumulates indefinitely, creating an unbounded memory leak.
findings.location: What > Async job API section: 'Job state is in-memory'
findings.suggested_fix: Use a fixed-size `collections.OrderedDict` (e.g., 1 000 entries) as the job store; on insert, if at capacity, discard the oldest completed/error record. Document that a 404 on a stale job_id means the client should resubmit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document and test the client's behavior on network errors during polling (e.g. retry transient failures up to N times, emit the job_id to stderr on give-up so the user can re-query manually, exit non-zero).
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the client's behavior when the server is unreachable during polling (after a successful 202 submission) — a likely non-happy-path in a remote, network-dependent async flow.
findings.location: Client section and AC: 'polls to a terminal status'; AC for offline default only covers the case where opcert_remote_url is unset

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly how human operators and administrators authenticate to the opcert service for operational access (e.g., 'admins reach the container exclusively via SSM Session Manager; no direct inbound human-auth path is needed' OR name an identity mechanism like IAM Identity Center/OIDC that gates human access at API Gateway).
findings.finding: The plan stands up a network-reachable FastAPI opcert service and specifies only machine-to-machine auth mechanisms (SigV4 at API Gateway, X-Opcert-Guard shared-secret header); it does not state how human operators or administrators authenticate to the service for operational access (triage, health checks, direct invocation). The IaC story is called out as handling API Gateway, but no explicit justified rationale covers admin access (e.g., 'admins access the service exclusively via SSM Session Manager on the container host; no direct inbound admin path exists').
findings.location: What section — Service subsection: 'Endpoint authn (SigV4) is terminated at API Gateway (the IaC story); the app additionally requires a shared secret as defense in depth'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single sentence to the Service section: 'Administrative access to the container host is exclusively via AWS SSM Session Manager (the existing AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore policy); no direct inbound human-auth path to the opcert app port is exposed outside the API Gateway + SigV4 perimeter.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability section to the plan specifying at minimum: a structured log sink (e.g., stdout JSON shipped to CloudWatch Logs), a health/readiness endpoint, and at least one alarm (e.g., job error-rate or queue depth) for the opcert service.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no observability (logging, metrics, or alarms) for the opcert service. The existing `monitoring.tf` covers the Gerrit instance and its gate health; no CloudWatch metric namespace, log group, health endpoint, or alarm is described for the new opcert FastAPI service, leaving its operational health invisible after deploy.
findings.location: What / Service section; infra/terraform/monitoring.tf (existing monitoring scope)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the exact boto3 call (`ssm.get_parameter(Name=..., WithDecryption=True)`) in the plan and state the required IAM action (`ssm:GetParameter` on the key ARN + `kms:Decrypt` on the CMK) as a precondition that the IaC story must satisfy.
findings.finding: The plan specifies the worker fetches the SSM SecureString at `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` before each job, but names neither the boto3 API call nor the required IAM action/policy. `ssm:GetParameter` with `WithDecryption=True` on that specific path is a distinct IAM permission from the CloudWatch `PutMetricData` boto3 usage already in the codebase, and the permission must be pre-provisioned on the service role. The plan delegates key provisioning details to the 'AWS IaC + deploy' story, but the worker code in THIS story calls SSM directly — an unverified new endpoint surface.
findings.location: Service section: 'the worker fetches the SSM SecureString (`/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`)'; Scope: 'SSM-parameter key materialization'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `verify.opcert_remote_url` must be validated to require an `https://` scheme at config-load time (reject or warn on `http://`) and document this constraint in the `VerifyConfig` field definition.
findings.finding: The plan does not state that verify.opcert_remote_url must use an HTTPS scheme. SigV4 signing provides request integrity and authentication but relies on TLS for confidentiality and replay protection; used over plain HTTP the Authorization header and request body are exposed in transit and the guard header (X-Opcert-Guard) would be sent in cleartext.
findings.location: Client section: 'Reads verify.opcert_remote_url (new VerifyConfig key, str | None = None)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the VerifyConfig validator (Pydantic), add: `@validator('opcert_remote_url') def must_be_https(cls, v): if v and not v.startswith('https://'): raise ValueError('opcert_remote_url must use https://'); return v` — or use `AnyHttpsUrl` as the field type.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a plan note (and a corresponding AC assertion or implementation note) that the `X-Opcert-Guard` header value MUST NOT appear in any server log output, and that any exception handler or logging middleware must redact or omit it.
findings.finding: The X-Opcert-Guard shared-secret header value is not declared as excluded from server access/error logs. FastAPI/uvicorn do not log request headers by default, but exception tracebacks and debug-mode middleware (e.g., uvicorn --log-level debug, custom error handlers, AWS CloudWatch structured logging from app code) can capture full header dictionaries. The plan has no instruction to redact or omit this header from any logging path.
findings.location: What section: 'the opcert app checks an X-Opcert-Guard header instead (value from the REBAR_OPCERT_GUARD env var)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the guard middleware, compare the header value but never log it; if logging the rejection, emit only 'X-Opcert-Guard header missing or invalid' (no value). Add a test that injects a known guard value, triggers a guard rejection, and asserts the value does not appear in captured log output.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the isinstance check order for the `error.class` mapping rule: most-specific subclass first (`LLMUnavailableError` → `"llm_unavailable"` checked before the `LLMError` catch-all → `"llm_error"`), to prevent subclass mis-routing.
findings.finding: The `error.class` exception-to-enum mapping does not specify isinstance check order. `LLMConfigError` is a subclass of `LLMUnavailableError` which is also a subclass of `LLMError` — an implementer who checks `isinstance(exc, LLMError)` before `isinstance(exc, LLMUnavailableError)` would route `LLMConfigError` (and `LLMUnavailableError` itself) into `"llm_error"` instead of `"llm_unavailable"`. The plan states the mapping but not the evaluation order.
findings.location: What section — Service, error.class closed enum; also AC line: 'a raised `LLMError` yields `status="error"` with `verdict=null`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the error.class definition: 'Checks are ordered most-specific first: `LLMUnavailableError` (and its subclasses, e.g. `LLMConfigError`) → `"llm_unavailable"`; any other `LLMError` subclass → `"llm_error"`; timeout → `"timeout"`; anything else → `"internal"`'.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
